Idiom (Toba Batak): Ingkon songon poting, lam marisi lam so marsoara
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed comparative proverb; modal obligation + simile clause + parallel comparative clauses.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A knowledgeable or mature person should become more measured, humble, and careful in speech.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sai unang gumodang hata molo nunga godang parbinotoan.
Usage Contexts: Family advice; education; leadership; church or community counsel.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Umpama ni halak Batak Toba; uses the image of a filled poting/container that makes less noise to praise humility after gaining wisdom.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Toba Batak): /iŋ.kon so.ŋon po.tiŋ | lam ma.ri.si lam so mar.so.a.ra/
First Example Sentence: Dung gabe panurat si Binsar, dipaingot amangna, “Ingkon songon poting, lam marisi lam so marsoara,” asa unang balga rohana.
Second Example Sentence: Di punguan marga, angka na pistar didok ingkon songon poting, lam marisi lam so marsoara.
Third Example Sentence: Molo marsiajar ho sahat tu ginjang, ingot ma: ingkon songon poting, lam marisi lam so marsoara.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed declarative clause; adjective predicate + genitive noun phrase with emphatic particle do.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A lie or hidden wrongdoing will eventually be exposed, however carefully it is covered.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Hasalaan dohot hata na so sintong pasti tarida di ujungna.
Usage Contexts: Warnings; family discipline; community disputes; moral teaching.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Umpama ni pargaulan ari-ari; the short legs of the gabus fish symbolize something that cannot be hidden for long.
First Example Sentence: Unang sai tatutup ho hata na so sintong, ai jempek do pat ni gabus.
Second Example Sentence: Dung ditungkun angka natoras, sandiri ibana mangaku, ala jempek do pat ni gabus.
Third Example Sentence: Sai tarida do pangalaho na sala i; jempek do pat ni gabus.

Idiom (Toba Batak): Jolo dinilat bibir asa nidok hata
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed imperative-like advisory clause; temporal adverb + passive verb phrase + purpose clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Think carefully before speaking so that words do not wound, shame, or create conflict.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Pikkiri jolo hata asa denggan nidok.
Usage Contexts: Daily conversation; mediation; adat speech; parent-to-child advice.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common Batak Toba speech ethic; the mouth/body image frames careful speaking as social self-control.
First Example Sentence: Di jolo ni hula-hula, jolo dinilat bibir asa nidok hata.
Second Example Sentence: Molo muruk roham, jolo dinilat bibir asa nidok hata, asa unang gabe parbadaan.
Third Example Sentence: Dipaingot inangna dakdanak i, “Jolo dinilat bibir asa nidok hata.”

Idiom (Toba Batak): Molo litok aek di toruan, tingkiron ma tu julu
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional proverb; if-clause + passive/imperative main clause with upstream/downstream spatial contrast.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When a problem appears at the surface, investigate its root cause instead of blaming the visible result.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Alusi jolo mula ni parsoalan asa boi dipature.
Usage Contexts: Conflict resolution; governance; workplace analysis; family counseling.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in Lake Toba and river imagery; downstream muddy water points speakers back to the upstream source.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Toba Batak): /mo.lo li.tok a.ek di to.ru.an | tiŋ.ki.ron ma tu dʒu.lu/
First Example Sentence: Di punguan i disukkun jolo mula ni parbada, ala molo litok aek di toruan, tingkiron ma tu julu.
Second Example Sentence: Unang holan pasalaon na menek; molo litok aek di toruan, tingkiron ma tu julu.
Third Example Sentence: Dung sai marsahit angka naposo, didok ompung, molo litok aek di toruan, tingkiron ma tu julu.

Idiom (Toba Batak): Songon parlange ni si bagur, tio tu jolo, litok di pudi
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed simile; comparative marker songon + nominal phrase + antithetical locative predicates.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone appears clean in front but leaves disorder, trouble, or unresolved harm behind.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Unang manadingkon parsoalan di pudi dung lao sian sada inganan.
Usage Contexts: Politics; workplace exits; neighborhood talk; sarcasm; moral critique.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Animal-water imagery in Batak Toba umpama; the swimming frog leaves clear water ahead and muddy water behind.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Toba Batak): /so.ŋon par.la.ŋe ni si ba.ɡur | ti.o tu dʒo.lo | li.tok di pu.di/
First Example Sentence: Unang songon parlange ni si bagur, tio tu jolo, litok di pudi, lao ho sian ulaon on.
Second Example Sentence: Ibana manadingkon barita na rumit di huta i, songon parlange ni si bagur, tio tu jolo, litok di pudi.
Third Example Sentence: Molo holan denggan di adopan alai marbahen susa di pudi, i do songon parlange ni si bagur, tio tu jolo, litok di pudi.

Idiom (Toba Batak): Tongka do mulak tata naung masak, mulak marimbulu naung tinutungan
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative-prohibitive proverb; modal prohibition + parallel perfective clauses.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: What has already happened cannot be undone; regret is less useful than repairing the consequences.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ulaon naung masa ndang taruba be; sipatureon ma akibatna.
Usage Contexts: Regret; reconciliation; legal or family decisions; post-conflict advice.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Domestic and hearth imagery; cooked food and burned feathers evoke irreversible acts in traditional household life.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Toba Batak): /toŋ.ka do mu.lak ta.ta na.uŋ ma.sak | mu.lak ma.rim.bu.lu na.uŋ ti.nu.tu.ŋan/
First Example Sentence: Dung masa hata na jorbut i, marsomba ma hita, ai tongka do mulak tata naung masak, mulak marimbulu naung tinutungan.
Second Example Sentence: Unang sai tangis di angka naung salpu; tongka do mulak tata naung masak, mulak marimbulu naung tinutungan.
Third Example Sentence: Ia mamodai pahoppuna asa manat, alana tongka do mulak tata naung masak, mulak marimbulu naung tinutungan.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative declarative; negator + adjective predicate + source prepositional phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Children, descendants, or outcomes often resemble their parents, origins, or roots.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Pangalaho ni anak jotjot songon pangalaho ni natorasna.
Usage Contexts: Family talk; genealogy; humor; character judgment; parenting.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agrarian plant metaphor; tubis/rebung does not grow far from its base, aligning behavior with lineage and origin.
First Example Sentence: Marnida panortoran ni dakdanak i, didok tulangna, ndang dao tubis sian bonana.
Second Example Sentence: Lambok hata ni boru i songon inangna; tutu do ndang dao tubis sian bonana.
Third Example Sentence: Molo pintar anak i marsiajar, ndang halongangan be, ndang dao tubis sian bonana.

Idiom (Toba Batak): Aek godang, aek laut; dos ni roha sibahen nasaut
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Paratactic proverb; nominal parallelism + equative clause linking consensus to completion.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Collective agreement, not individual stubbornness, is what makes a decision valid and workable.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Na sada roha do na patut dilaksanahon.
Usage Contexts: Adat deliberation; meetings; family decisions; cooperative work.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Water convergence metaphor in Batak Toba consensus culture; streams and sea frame agreement as social completion.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Toba Batak): /a.ek ɡo.daŋ a.ek la.ut | dos ni ro.ha si.ba.hen na.sa.ut/
First Example Sentence: Dung ditimbang sude hata, aek godang, aek laut; dos ni roha sibahen nasaut.
Second Example Sentence: Di ulaon adat, ndang holan sada roha na mangatur, aek godang, aek laut; dos ni roha sibahen nasaut.
Third Example Sentence: Rap hita mamutuskon tangkas, ai aek godang, aek laut; dos ni roha sibahen nasaut.

Idiom (Toba Batak): Piltik ni hasapi do tabo tu pinggol, anggo piltik ni hata sogo do begeon
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Balanced comparative proverb; genitive noun phrase + evaluative predicate + contrastive subordinate clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Pleasant art is worth hearing, but gossip, slander, or destructive talk should be avoided.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Unang bege manang pasahathon hata na mangarusa goar ni dongan.
Usage Contexts: Music gatherings; social correction; church/community advice; anti-gossip speech.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: References hasapi, a Batak lute; contrasts beautiful sound with damaging speech in oral community life.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Toba Batak): /pil.tik ni ha.sa.pi do ta.bo tu piŋ.ɡol | aŋ.ɡo pil.tik ni ha.ta so.ɡo do be.ɡe.on/
First Example Sentence: Di lapo i, didok amangboru, piltik ni hasapi do tabo tu pinggol, anggo piltik ni hata sogo do begeon.
Second Example Sentence: Unang pasahathon hata na mangarusa dongan; piltik ni hasapi do tabo tu pinggol, anggo piltik ni hata sogo do begeon.
Third Example Sentence: Asa dame punguan, dipasahat uluan hata i: piltik ni hasapi do tabo tu pinggol, anggo piltik ni hata sogo do begeon.

Idiom (Toba Batak): Naso matanggak di hata, naso matahut di bohi
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical parallel relative construction; repeated negative relative phrase with locative complements.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person should speak truth plainly and courageously without boasting or fearing faces of authority.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Hatahon na sintong jala patuduhon na sala, unang gumodang hata manang mabiar.
Usage Contexts: Leadership; ethics; court-like deliberation; political speech; character education.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Batak Toba moral philosophy about truth-telling; used in leadership and character education discourse.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Toba Batak): /na.so ma.taŋ.ɡak di ha.ta | na.so ma.ta.hut di bo.hi/
First Example Sentence: Di jolo ni raja adat, ingkon naso matanggak di hata, naso matahut di bohi.
Second Example Sentence: Ia marsihohot tu na sintong, naso matanggak di hata, naso matahut di bohi do ibana.
Third Example Sentence: Guru i mamodai hami asa naso matanggak di hata, naso matahut di bohi molo mangkatahon hasintongan.
